questions.q: Deadlock requires four conditions: mutual exclusion, hold-and-wait, no preemption, and circular wait. Breaking which condition is the simplest and most practical in most systems?
options: No preemption—allow the OS to forcibly take locks from threads
Hold-and-wait—acquire all locks needed before proceeding, or use lock-free mechanisms
Circular wait—establish a total ordering of locks and acquire them in order
Mutual exclusion—remove all locks and use only atomic operations
questions.explanation: Breaking hold-and-wait by acquiring all locks upfront or releasing locks before acquiring new ones prevents the scenario where thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2 while B does the opposite. Lock-free algorithms also help. Removing mutual exclusion is impractical for protecting shared data; enforced lock ordering is also practical but requires discipline. OS-level preemption is rarely available for application locks.

questions.q: Two threads call lock(A) then lock(B) in order, but they call lock(A) with timeouts. Thread X times out waiting for A, releases any locks it holds, and retries. Thread Y times out waiting for A the same way. What is this scenario called?
options: Deadlock
Livelock
Starvation
Priority inversion
questions.explanation: This is livelock: both threads are executing (not blocked), but neither makes progress. They repeatedly acquire and release locks in a cycle without accomplishing their goal. Deadlock would be both blocked permanently. Starvation is one thread never getting a resource. Priority inversion involves priority scheduling anomalies.

questions.q: A classic deadlock example: Thread A holds lock 1 and waits for lock 2; Thread B holds lock 2 and waits for lock 1. This situation satisfies all four deadlock conditions. How many of the four conditions must be broken to guarantee deadlock cannot occur?
options: One condition must be broken
At least two conditions must be broken
All four conditions must be broken
The number depends on which conditions are broken
questions.explanation: Breaking just one of the four conditions is sufficient to prevent deadlock. For instance, imposing a lock order (breaks circular wait), requiring all locks upfront (breaks hold-and-wait), or implementing timeouts with retries (approximates breaking no-preemption) will prevent the deadlock. You do not need to break all four; one is enough.

questions.q: Thread priorities can lead to priority inversion: a high-priority thread waits for a lock held by a low-priority thread that cannot run because a medium-priority thread is running. How is priority inheritance used to prevent this?
options: The high-priority thread is promoted to skip the queue
The low-priority lock holder temporarily inherits the high-priority thread's priority while holding the lock
The medium-priority thread is paused until the lock is released
The OS removes priority levels and uses only FIFO scheduling
questions.explanation: Priority inheritance elevates the lock holder's priority to at least that of the highest-priority waiter. This allows the low-priority thread to preempt the medium-priority thread and release the lock quickly, unblocking the high-priority waiter. Once the lock is released, priorities revert. This prevents the inversion without queue manipulation.

questions.q: A resource allocation graph shows processes as nodes and resource allocations as edges. An edge from resource R to process P means P holds R; an edge from P to R means P is waiting for R. A deadlock exists if and only if there is a cycle with what additional property?
options: All processes in the cycle have the same priority
At least one resource in the cycle is held by only one process
The cycle involves only reusable resources, not consumable resources
The cycle does not have a way to be resolved without external intervention
questions.explanation: A cycle in the resource allocation graph indicates a potential deadlock. However, if a resource has multiple instances and is held by one process while another waits, the cycle may be resolved when the holder releases it. Deadlock is only guaranteed when no process in the cycle can complete (all are waiting on something in the cycle). The specific property is that the cycle is irreversible without external action.

questions.q: Banker's algorithm prevents deadlock by refusing resource requests that would lead to an unsafe state. What defines an 'unsafe state'?
options: A state where some process cannot complete even if other processes release their resources immediately
A state where the system has more processes than resources
A state where all locks are held by high-priority threads
A state where deadlock has already occurred
questions.explanation: An unsafe state is one where there is no safe sequence of process execution that allows all to eventually complete. The algorithm checks whether granting a request would lead to such a state and denies the request if so. This is a preventive strategy that may refuse some valid requests to ensure deadlock-free execution.

questions.q: Starvation can occur when a high-priority thread repeatedly acquires a lock, preventing a low-priority thread from ever acquiring it. How does a fair or FIFO lock mitigate starvation?
options: It promotes low-priority threads to high-priority when waiting
It grants the lock to the thread that has waited the longest, regardless of priority
It allows multiple threads to hold the lock simultaneously
It uses random selection to give all threads an equal chance
questions.explanation: A fair or FIFO lock grants the lock in the order requests were made, not by priority or random selection. This ensures that a low-priority thread waiting first will not be starved by repeated high-priority acquisitions. Once a thread is first in the queue, it will eventually receive the lock.

questions.q: Two threads try to acquire locks in different orders: Thread A locks X then Y; Thread B locks Y then X. Even if both threads succeed and hold their locks without waiting, are they in deadlock?
options: Yes, because a circular wait exists and the deadlock conditions are satisfied
No, because both threads hold their locks and are not blocked waiting for anything
Yes, because circular wait is a necessary condition that must be checked statically
No, they are progressing and will eventually complete
questions.explanation: Deadlock is a runtime state, not a static pattern. A circular wait condition alone does not guarantee deadlock if no thread is actually waiting. In this scenario, both threads hold their locks and will eventually release them, so no deadlock occurs. The ordering issue becomes a problem only if they try to acquire locks concurrently in conflicting orders.

questions.q: A thread acquires lock A, releases it, acquires lock B, and then tries to re-acquire lock A. This pattern is called 'hold-and-wait' and violates the hold-and-wait condition, but can it still deadlock?
options: No, because the thread releases locks before acquiring new ones
Yes, if another thread has acquired A after it was released
Yes, because hold-and-wait is the only condition necessary for deadlock
No, deadlock requires all four conditions, and breaking hold-and-wait guarantees safety
questions.explanation: Even though this thread avoids the classic hold-and-wait scenario, it can still deadlock if another thread is in a circular wait with it (e.g., B is held by thread 2 which is waiting for A). Breaking hold-and-wait reduces the risk of deadlock but does not guarantee immunity. However, a more nuanced answer is that strategic lock ordering and short hold times minimize the window for circular wait.

questions.q: Deadlock detection algorithms periodically check the resource allocation graph for cycles. If a cycle is detected, what action should be taken?
options: Immediately terminate all processes in the cycle without recovery
Preempt and roll back one or more processes to break the cycle, then restart them
Ignore the cycle and let it resolve naturally
Switch to a lock-free algorithm dynamically
questions.explanation: Once deadlock is detected, the system must break the cycle by preempting and rolling back one or more processes. This may lose their progress, but it recovers the system from deadlock. Immediate termination without recovery is destructive. Ignoring the cycle is not an option—the processes will remain blocked forever. Dynamic algorithm switching is impractical during execution.
